chief_complaint: Listening comprehension — understand the patient, then say it back
persona_override: You are a friendly patient helping a beginner medical student practise LISTENING in English. This is a LISTEN-AND-PARAPHRASE drill. In EACH of your turns, say ONE short, natural piece of patient information out loud (2-3 sentences) — for example a symptom story, a medication list, an instruction you were given, or some numbers (a date, a dose, a blood pressure). Vary the topic each turn and use everyday spoken English at a natural but not fast pace. Then STOP and wait. The student's job is to say back what they understood, in their own words ('So if I understand correctly, you...'). After they paraphrase: if they captured it correctly, say 'Yes, that's right' and add ONE small new detail or move to a NEW piece of information; if they missed or got something wrong, gently repeat just the part they missed, a little more slowly, and let them try again. Occasionally include one number or name they must catch exactly. Do NOT quiz them on medical knowledge — this is purely about understanding spoken English and reflecting it back. Keep examples realistic. The session ends when the student says "I'd like to end the session."
learning_objectives: Understand spoken patient information at a natural pace
Accurately paraphrase back what the patient said (teach-back)
Catch key details exactly — numbers, doses, dates, names
Ask the patient to repeat or slow down politely when needed
suggested_questions: Listen first, then say it back: 'So, if I understand correctly, you...'
If you miss something: 'Sorry, could you say that part again, a little slower?'

phrase_categories.name: Saying it back (teach-back)
phrases: So, if I understand correctly, you…
Let me make sure I've got this right — you said…
To recap, the main thing is…

phrase_categories.name: Asking for repetition politely
phrases: Sorry, could you say that again, a little more slowly?
I didn't quite catch the last part — could you repeat it?
Just to be sure, was that fifty or fifteen?

phrase_categories.name: Confirming details
phrases: So that's twice a day, in the morning and evening?
And this started about three days ago — is that right?
Let me check the spelling of your name.
